title: Young Americans Financial Literacy Act
stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Financial Services, and in addition to the Committee on Education and Workforce, for a peri
plain_summary: This bill creates a grant program that gives money to groups who want to teach young people about money and finances. The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau will give these grants to partnerships between schools, nonprofits, government agencies, and banks. These groups will use the money to create programs that teach kids and families ages eight through twenty-four how to manage money, avoid debt, and make smart financial choices. The program will get between twenty-seven and fifty-five million dollars each year until 2029.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Financial Services, and in addition to the Committee on Education and Workforce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Young Americans Financial Literacy Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings
sections.text: Congress has found the following facts about financial education in America. Eighty-eight percent of Americans think schools should teach financial education and ninety-two percent of teachers in kindergarten through twelfth grade agree, but only twelve percent of teachers actually teach this subject. According to a two thousand twenty survey, less than half of states require high school students to take a personal finance course. Less than seventeen percent of high school students were required to take a one semester personal finance class. For four years in a row, more than one third of people surveyed gave themselves a grade of B when rating their own basic financial knowledge. Less than one fifth of Americans gave themselves an A grade. Most adults feel their financial knowledge is not good enough, but they do not ask anyone else to handle their money. They think it is important to know more but have never received financial education. The sudden problems caused by COVID-19 are creating economic challenges with growing effects. While some things that affect financial well-being are beyond a person's control, financial knowledge can help people better manage their money during hard times. We need to respond immediately to the urgent needs of people who have lost their financial stability. However, we must also pay more attention to improving financial education and finding long-term solutions to prevent future personal financial disasters. There is an urgent need to respond to the COVID-19 economic recovery with research-based financial education programs. These programs should reach people of all ages and income levels, especially those facing unique and challenging financial situations. This includes high school graduates starting work, students about to graduate or who recently graduated from college, young families, and military personnel and their families who have special needs. High school and college students who receive ongoing financial education show an increase in financial knowledge. This knowledge then leads to more responsible behavior as they become young adults. The majority, fifty-two percent, of young adults between ages twenty-three and twenty-eight consider making better choices about managing money the single most important issue for individual Americans to act on today. According to the Government Accountability Office, giving Americans the information they need to make good financial decisions can be key to their well-being and to the country's economic health. The current pandemic, in which eighty-eight percent of Americans say is causing stress on their personal finances, shows the need to improve people's financial knowledge and help all Americans make informed financial decisions. This is especially true for young people as they are earning their first paychecks, getting student aid, and becoming financially independent. Therefore, focusing economic education and financial literacy efforts and best practices for young people between ages eight and twenty-four is extremely important.

sections.title: Section 3 Authorization for Funding the Establishment of Centers of Excellence in Financial Literacy Education
sections.text: This section changes an existing law called the Consumer Financial Protection Act of two thousand ten. It moves section one thousand thirty-seven to become section one thousand thirty-eight and adds a new section one thousand thirty-seven. The new section gives the Director of the Bureau power to create a grant program. The Director must work with the Financial Literacy and Education Commission to make competitive grants to qualified institutions. These grants will help create centers of excellence that support research, development, planning, putting into practice, and evaluation of effective financial literacy education programs for young people and families ages eight through twenty-four years old. The grants can pay for several activities. First, developing and putting into practice complete research-based financial literacy education programs for young people. These programs must be based on core skills and concepts set by the Director, including goal setting, planning, budgeting, managing money or transactions, tools and structures, behaviors, consequences, both long-term and short-term savings, managing debt and earnings. The programs must be able to fit into educational settings through existing academic subjects, including materials that properly serve various groups of at-risk populations, particularly minority and disadvantaged individuals. Second, designing instructional materials using evidence-based content for young families and conducting related outreach activities to address unique life situations and financial problems, including bankruptcy, foreclosure, credit card misuse, and predatory lending. Third, developing and supporting professional development programs in financial literacy education to ensure competence and accountability in the delivery system. Fourth, improving access to and sharing of financial literacy information for young people and families. Fifth, reducing student loan default rates by developing programs to help individuals better understand how to manage educational debt through ongoing educational programs for college students. Sixth, conducting ongoing research and evaluation of financial literacy education programs to ensure learning of defined skills and knowledge and keeping that learning. Seventh, developing research-based assessment and accountability of the appropriate uses of learning over short and long terms to measure effectiveness of authorized activities. The Director must give priority to applications that provide clear definitions of financial literacy and financially literate to clarify educational outcomes. Priority also goes to applications that establish rules for identifying the types of programs that most effectively reach young people and families in unique life situations and financial problems, including bankruptcy, foreclosure, credit card misuse, and predatory lending. Other priority factors include content that is appropriate to age and income levels, programs based on educational standards, definitions, and research, individual goals of financial independence and stability, professional development and delivery systems using evidence-based practices, addressing the needs of one or more at-risk populations, incorporating awareness of specific cultural, linguistic, or demographic characteristics, enhancing opportunities for asset building such as increasing savings for lower income households and investments into the stock, bond, and real estate markets, including an evaluation part to ensure the work's effectiveness in increasing financial literacy or consumer access to appropriate financial products or services or that the provider has evidence of such effectiveness, promising future replication or sustainability beyond the program period, and making effectiveness data that is generated from the work available to others in the financial education community. The Director must establish application and evaluation standards and procedures, distribution criteria, and such other forms, standards, definitions, and procedures as the Director determines to be appropriate. Any qualified institution receiving a grant under this section must ensure that content is delivered in an accessible way to young people through traditional educational methods and digital methods, including over appropriate social media platforms. To the extent content is delivered through a website, the institution must ensure that the website is user friendly, visually appealing, and does not overwhelm users with dense content that is difficult to understand. The total amount of grants made under this section during any fiscal year must be at least twenty-seven million five hundred thousand dollars and may not exceed fifty-five million dollars. No grants may be made under this section after the end of fiscal year two thousand twenty-nine. The Director must issue an annual report to Congress containing a list of grant recipients under this section, including the amount of such grant, and for each grant recipient, a description of the specific populations being served by such grant. For purposes of this section, qualified institution means a partnership of two or more of the following: an institution of higher education, a state or local government agency which specializes in financial education programs, a nonprofit agency, organization, or association, a financial institution, or a small organization that is partnering with, but is not itself, one of the previously mentioned types of organizations. Institution of higher education has the meaning given in section one hundred one of the Higher Education Act of nineteen sixty-five. The section also makes a clerical amendment to update the table of contents in the Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act to reflect the new section numbering.
